title: AI Playbook - Subject Leaders
subtitle: Practical, role-aware guidance for school principals, foundation representatives, academic coordinators, and Cambridge coordinators at Eduversal partner schools

audience._note: All four leadership sub-roles read this playbook in full. Role-specific sections are flagged with audienceScope. Foundation Representatives focus heavily on Strategy + Policy; Principals focus on whole-school integration; Academic Coordinators focus on Teaching & Learning + Staff Capability; Cambridge Coordinators focus on Cambridge-syllabus alignment + exams integrity.

_meta.purpose: What a school leader actually needs: practical guidance to lead AI integration in your school without either over-promising or stalling. Every block is anchored to a citable reference — no invented best practices.
_meta.referenceLayer: ../reference/ — verbatim Eduversal v1.0 (especially Part 3 Institutional AI Maturity) + UNESCO + external sources.
_meta.doNotFabricate: Every workflow / decision-aid / red-flag carries a provenance object. Lint enforces non-empty provenance.

howToUseThisPlaybook.firstWeek: Read 'orientation' + 'redFlags' + skim 'institutionalMaturity'. Understand which of the 5 maturity levels your school is at (most schools in 2026 are Level 1-2; honesty matters more than ambition here).
howToUseThisPlaybook.firstMonth: Run a baseline conversation with your Principal + Academic Coordinator on each of the 6 institutional domains. Write down where you actually are — not where you wish you were.
howToUseThisPlaybook.firstTerm: Pick TWO domains to advance one level. Not six. Two. Cambridge's own 2026 digital maturity refresh emphasises gradual systemic change over flagship initiatives.
howToUseThisPlaybook.ongoing: Use 'coreWorkflows' for the recurring leadership work: policy decisions, staff capability building, parent communication, network reporting.

orientation.what_this_is: AI integration is now a school-leadership accountability, not just a teacher-skills question. The Eduversal Academic Directorate uses Part 3 of the framework (Institutional AI Maturity) during appraisal visits. Your job is not to make your school 'Level 5 Leading' overnight — it's to make honest, evidenced progress year over year.
what_changed_for_leaders_in_2026: Cambridge International's Feb 2026 curriculum refresh redefines 'digital maturity' for the AI age — this affects your Cambridge-syllabus alignment, your exams policies, and your prep for Cambridge inspection cycles.
Permendikdasmen 13/2025 lets schools offer Koding & AI as electives starting 2025/2026 academic year. Schools self-assess readiness — your decision whether and when to offer it is a strategic one.

UU PDP No. 27/2022 is fully enforceable since Oct 2024. Your school carries the data-protection compliance burden for any AI tool used with student data. The Lembaga PDP enforcement body is on track for 2026.

No Indonesian Permendikdasmen regulation on AI-USE (teacher conduct) exists yet. The Eduversal AI Competency Framework v1.0 fills this gap for your school — you are operating under network policy, not ministry policy, on this question.
orientation.the_leadership_question_to_keep_asking: Does our school's AI use enhance professional judgement and student learning, or substitute for them? Every policy decision routes back to this.

institutionalMaturity.title: Reading your school's current AI maturity honestly
institutionalMaturity.philosophy: The Eduversal Institutional AI Maturity Framework (Part 3) uses 5 levels (Emerging → Developing → Established → Advanced → Leading) across 6 domains. Most Eduversal schools in 2026 honestly sit at Level 1-2 on most domains. That is normal. Inflating your self-assessment hurts you twice: first when the Academic Directorate appraisal contradicts it, and second when you set targets you can't meet.

sixDomainsSummary.strategy_leadership: Do you have an actual AI strategy document, or just intentions? Does it appear in school improvement planning? Has leadership communicated clear expectations to staff?
sixDomainsSummary.policy_compliance: Do you have written AI policies (staff use + student use + data privacy + academic integrity)? Are they understood? Are they actually followed?
sixDomainsSummary.staff_capability: What percentage of teaching staff have achieved Foundation level competency? Is there a professional development programme, or just ad-hoc training?
sixDomainsSummary.teaching_learning: Is AI integration consistent across departments and year groups? Is student AI literacy curriculum implemented systematically? Can you show evidence that AI integration enhances learning outcomes?
sixDomainsSummary.student_outcomes: Can students at each age band demonstrate the expected AI literacy strands? Is responsible AI use established as a school norm?
sixDomainsSummary.infrastructure_resources: Does your technical infrastructure support AI integration reliably? Is there approved-tools provisioning, budget allocation, technical support capacity?

selfAssessmentCadence.frequency: Annual, at the start of academic year
selfAssessmentCadence.honesty: If you're Level 2 on Staff Capability, mark Level 2. The appraisal team has cross-school comparison data; inflated ratings are detected immediately.

institutionalMaturity.twoDomainRule: Pick TWO domains per academic year to advance one level. Not six. Schools that try to advance everything at once advance nothing.

strategicPlanningAndPolicy.title: Setting AI strategy and policy that staff can actually follow
strategicPlanningAndPolicy.philosophy: An AI strategy that lives in a PDF nobody reads is worse than no strategy. The test is whether a Year 7 teacher could correctly answer 'what does our school say about AI use?' two months after policy adoption.

steps.title: Start with the policy, not the strategy
steps.do: Write the simplest possible AI policy first: what tools are approved, what's prohibited, what data may not be entered, what disclosure is expected from staff and students. One page. Plain language.
steps.why: Strategy without policy generates aspiration without compliance. Policy without strategy is fine for 6 months.

steps.title: Reference the regulatory anchors explicitly
steps.do: Your policy must reference: UU PDP No. 27/2022 (data protection), Cambridge International coursework guidance (academic integrity for Cambridge work), and Eduversal AI Competency Framework v1.0 (network expectations). Don't paraphrase — cite.
steps.why: When a parent or auditor questions a decision, you point at the cited source, not at your own judgement.

steps.title: Decide on approved tools
steps.do: Pick ONE tool tier per category: one approved AI for teacher productivity (likely a school-tier product like ChatGPT for Teachers, Claude for Education, or Gemini for Education), and decisions about student-facing tool access. Document the data-handling contract.
steps.why: Free-tier consumer accounts and personal accounts default to using your data for model training. School-tier products have contractually different data handling. Without an approved-tools list, staff will improvise — usually with free tiers.

steps.title: Communicate it, then communicate it again
steps.do: Staff briefing → all-staff written summary → department-level Q&A → policy embedded in induction. Then refresh quarterly. Cambridge's Feb 2026 refresh assumes AI policy is COMMUNICATED, not just adopted.
steps.why: Policy adoption ≠ policy understanding. Survey staff six weeks later; the gap will surprise you.

steps.title: Review annually
steps.do: AI tools and regulatory environment will both change. Annual review of policy is mandatory, not optional.
steps.why: Last year's policy will be wrong somewhere by next year. Reviews catch the wrongness early.

antiPatterns: Don't outsource policy drafting to an AI tool with no expert review. Policy is one of the few things where AI drafting is high-risk: it reads plausibly while missing local regulatory context (especially Indonesian UU PDP specifics).
Don't write policy that bans AI without offering alternatives. Bans without provision push use underground.
Don't write policy that says 'use AI responsibly' without defining what responsible looks like. Vague policy = no policy.

toolsForThisWorkflow._note: Policy drafting is high-risk for AI use. Use AI for structure and first drafts; expert review on substance.
toolsForThisWorkflow.primary: Claude for Education — most cautious tone of the core three, best for sensitive policy language. Tends to flag uncertainty rather than fill gaps with confident-sounding fabrication.
toolsForThisWorkflow.secondaryDocumentAnalysis: NotebookLM — load Cambridge handbooks, Permendiknas, Eduversal framework docs into one notebook for cross-referencing during policy drafting. Strong at finding contradictions across documents.
toolsForThisWorkflow.doNotUse: Free / personal tier of any tool. Policy work touches confidential institutional content.

staffCapabilityBuilding.title: Moving staff from Foundation toward Practitioner
staffCapabilityBuilding.philosophy: Eduversal v1.0 expects all teaching staff to reach Foundation level within Year 1 of framework implementation, and the majority of staff to reach Practitioner within Year 2. These are realistic only with deliberate professional development, not osmosis.

patterns.how: Use the annual self-assessment cycle (Phase 2c). Don't guess where staff are. Aggregate by department and year-of-experience cohort.
patterns.why: You'll find that AI capability does NOT correlate with years of teaching experience. Targeting PD by experience-cohort wastes resources.

patterns.how: Three 30-minute workshops over three weeks beats one 90-minute session every time. Teachers retain a workflow they try the next day; they forget a workflow demonstrated to them once.
patterns.why: Practitioner-pattern from 2025-2026 — Anthropic + OECD both note that hands-on iteration beats lecture-style AI PD.

patterns.pattern: Lean on teacher leaders, not external consultants
patterns.how: Identify your 2-3 strongest practitioner-level teachers. Their leadership of small PD slots scales faster than buying external training.
patterns.why: Internal teacher leaders carry credibility, subject-context knowledge, and continuity across the year.

patterns.how: When introducing AI to a hesitant teacher, start with differentiation. It's the workflow with the biggest time-to-impact ratio and the lowest red-flag risk.
patterns.why: Differentiation is the #1 teacher AI use case in 2025-2026 (~61% per Anthropic's educator usage report). It builds confidence.

patterns.how: Phase 2d gives you line-manager validation of teacher self-assessments. If a self-declared Practitioner doesn't show Practitioner evidence, say so. Performative level inflation undermines the whole framework.
patterns.why: Cross-school comparison data shows when inflation is happening. Honest assessment protects your school's standing in network appraisal.

antiPatterns: Don't mandate AI use as a performance metric. Teachers who feel surveilled into using AI use it badly or fake it.
Don't run AI PD without follow-up coaching. One-shot training has near-zero impact on classroom practice.
Don't ignore the 10-20% of staff who genuinely cannot or will not engage with AI. Address it through normal performance management routes; don't let it derail school-wide progress.

toolsForThisWorkflow._note: Tools that help YOU train staff and run capability-building work.
toolsForThisWorkflow.forTrainingMaterials: Canva for Education + Magic Studio — make staff training slides, infographics, capability summary posters. Safest school-tier choice.
toolsForThisWorkflow.forStaffMeetingTranscription: Otter.ai (Pro tier minimum for staff PD content). Lets you focus on the training rather than note-taking.
toolsForThisWorkflow.forStaffPdResources: MagicSchool — has a 'Teacher Coach' tool that helps you build PD content; same login your teachers already use, lowers the barrier.
toolsForThisWorkflow.note: When you train staff on AI tools, demo using the tool your school has approved — not the tool you personally prefer. Inconsistency confuses staff.

parentAndCommunityCommunication.title: Talking to parents and community about AI in your school
parentAndCommunityCommunication.philosophy: Parents fall into three groups: anxious (worried about screen time, cheating, child safety), curious (wanting to know what their child is learning), and ahead-of-you (already using AI with their child at home, expecting the school to match). Your communication must work for all three.

patterns.pattern: One open letter at start of academic year
patterns.how: Plain-language letter to all parents covering: how teachers use AI (productivity, differentiation), how students use AI (under instruction, in age-appropriate ways), what we don't do (grade with AI, share student data), how to ask questions.
patterns.why: A single proactive communication prevents 50 reactive ones. Parents who feel informed don't panic.

patterns.pattern: Parent forum or Q&A once per year
patterns.how: 30-minute session, optional. Cover the same content as the letter, plus live questions. Have a teacher leader present, not just administration.
patterns.why: Anxious parents need to see a real teacher say 'we don't do that'. Letters can't do that.

patterns.how: AI in education is genuinely evolving. Your communication should say so. Phrases like 'we will review this approach annually' and 'our policy may change as Cambridge International publishes new guidance' build trust.
patterns.why: Pretending you have everything figured out is detected immediately and erodes credibility.

antiPatterns: Don't use AI to draft the parent communication WITHOUT editing into authentic school voice. Parents will detect generic AI tone and your message about responsible AI use will collapse on itself.
Don't promise that AI will improve outcomes if you have no evidence yet. Network-level evidence is still being aggregated (Phase 3b).

anchors: reference:eduversal-v1-part3-institutional.strategy_leadership.level3 (leadership regularly communicates expectations and celebrates progress)

toolsForThisWorkflow.primary: Claude for Education — most natural Bahasa Indonesia output for high-stakes parent letters per 2025-2026 benchmark.
toolsForThisWorkflow.forBilingualParentLetters: DeepL Pro — best dedicated translator for EN ↔ Bahasa parent communication. Better register than Google Translate per Indonesian academic studies.
toolsForThisWorkflow.forFormalSchoolWideLetters: Claude for Education or Gemini for Education for tone-aware drafts (Bahasa register strong on both), then DeepL Pro for verification pass on the Bahasa version.
toolsForThisWorkflow.doNotUse: Free Google Translate for high-stakes parent letters — register is often wrong (too formal or too casual) for the actual situation.

cambridgeSyllabusAlignment.title: Aligning AI integration with Cambridge International requirements
cambridgeSyllabusAlignment.philosophy: Eduversal partner schools are Cambridge International institutions. AI integration that conflicts with Cambridge requirements is not innovation — it's risk. Cambridge has been deliberately measured in its AI guidance and your alignment work tracks that pace, not a faster one.

patterns.pattern: Subscribe to the Cambridge AI hub updates
patterns.how: Cambridge maintains a live AI hub (linked in manifest externalAnchors). Designate one person to monitor it and report changes at termly Cambridge Coordinator meetings.
patterns.why: Cambridge does NOT publish a single standalone 'AI policy for schools' PDF. Guidance is dispersed across the hub + Exams Officers Guide. Active monitoring is required.

patterns.pattern: Coursework AI policy comes from Exams Officers Guide Phase 3
patterns.how: For any Cambridge syllabus with coursework component (IGCSE, AS/A Level), your coursework AI policy must defer to Cambridge's Exams Officers Guide Phase 3. Do not write parallel school policy that diverges from Cambridge's.
patterns.why: Coursework integrity questions are adjudicated by Cambridge, not by you. Your school policy can be MORE restrictive than Cambridge's, never less.

patterns.pattern: Map AI integration to Cambridge learner attributes
patterns.how: Cambridge's 'confident, responsible, reflective, innovative, engaged' learner attributes are the official curriculum framing. When you describe AI integration to Cambridge inspection teams, frame it through these attributes.
patterns.why: Cambridge inspection reports use these attributes. Your reporting language should match.

patterns.pattern: Watch the Feb 2026 Primary + Lower Secondary Digital Literacy refresh
patterns.how: Cambridge refreshed these curricula in Feb 2026 specifically to redefine 'digital maturity' for the AI era. Your Primary and Lower Secondary teachers need to know this. Termly briefing minimum.
patterns.why: Curriculum refreshes propagate slowly to classroom practice unless deliberately surfaced.

antiPatterns: Don't allow AI use in coursework that Cambridge prohibits — even if your school's general AI policy is more permissive.
Don't accuse a student of AI misuse in Cambridge coursework without following Cambridge's procedures. AI detection tools are unreliable and Cambridge has its own integrity processes.

toolsForThisWorkflow.forCambridgeBulletinAnalysis: NotebookLM — load multiple Cambridge bulletins and the syllabus into one notebook; ask cross-document questions. Strong for spotting what changed across versions.
toolsForThisWorkflow.forCambridgeBulletinDistribution: Claude for Education or ChatGPT for Teachers — draft termly briefings to Subject Leaders from a Cambridge bulletin. Always verify AI's summary against the original bulletin.
toolsForThisWorkflow.doNotUseForAiDetection: AI detection tools (Turnitin AI, GPTZero, etc.) are unsafe for Eduversal's EAL student population. 2023 Patterns study: 7 detectors flagged 61% of non-native English essays as AI-generated. Multiple major universities disabled Turnitin AI detection in late 2025 for this reason. Use Cambridge's own integrity procedures, not detector scores.

dataProtectionAndCompliance.title: UU PDP compliance for school AI use
dataProtectionAndCompliance.philosophy: Under UU PDP No. 27/2022, your school is the data controller for student personal data. AI tools that process that data are your processors. The compliance burden rests on the school, not on the teacher who pasted the data, not on the AI vendor.

patterns.how: Document, for each AI tool the school approves: vendor name, data-handling contract terms (is student data used for model training? where is data processed?), data residency (Indonesia or otherwise), and the categories of school data it may process.
patterns.why: When the Lembaga PDP enforcement body (target 2026) asks how your school handles AI processing, the register is your answer.

patterns.pattern: Prefer school-tier products over consumer tiers
patterns.how: Claude for Education, ChatGPT for Teachers, Gemini for Education all have contractually different data handling from their consumer-tier counterparts. Default to school-tier for any AI use that touches student context.
patterns.why: Free-tier and personal-tier accounts typically default to using conversations for model training. School-tier products typically don't. The contract matters.

patterns.pattern: Train staff on what is and isn't personal data
patterns.how: Annual training: name + school + class + grade are obviously personal data. A description of 'a Year 8 student with diagnosed dyslexia' is also identifiable if you teach a class with one such student. Stripped-down generic descriptors are safer.
patterns.why: Most data-protection breaches happen via teachers who don't realise their prompt was identifying.

patterns.how: Most AI tools process data outside Indonesia. UU PDP doesn't ban this but raises the burden of proof on transfer adequacy. Your approved-tools register should record where each tool processes data and what contractual safeguards apply.
patterns.why: If a transfer adequacy challenge arises, you need documentation.

antiPatterns: Don't treat 'free' as 'safe'. Free-tier AI tools typically pay for themselves with your data.
Don't allow staff to use personal-account AI for sustained school work involving student data. Cross-account use is invisible to school controls and unauditable.
Don't claim compliance you can't evidence. UU PDP enforcement will request documentation, not assurances.

approvedToolsRegisterTemplate._note: What your school's approved-tools register should contain for each tool, as required by UU PDP compliance posture.

perTool.name: e.g. Claude for Education
perTool.dataHandlingContract: Link to or copy of the DPA
perTool.dataResidency: US-hosted; cross-border transfer with documented adequacy safeguards
perTool.approvedFor: All teacher productivity work; HQ specialist curriculum drafting
perTool.notApprovedFor: Student-facing direct use (no teacher monitoring); confidential staff appraisal content

approvedToolsRegisterTemplate.minimumApprovedTools2026: Most Eduversal schools will approve: ONE general-purpose AI assistant from the core three (Claude / ChatGPT / Gemini for Education); ONE specialised differentiation tool (likely MagicSchool or Brisk Teaching); ONE translation tool for parent comms (DeepL Pro); ONE student-facing safe AI (likely SchoolAI). See manifest.json#toolRecommendations for the full menu.

redFlags._note: Network-wide unconditional NO for school leaders. No context shifts these.

items.rule: NEVER use AI as the basis for high-stakes decisions about a specific child (set placement, intervention plan, SEN referral, disciplinary action).
items.why: AI does not know your students. Even when described, the output is pattern-matching against generic profiles, not judgement about a child you serve.
items.safeAlternative: AI can summarise documented evidence. The decision belongs to the pastoral/SEN/safeguarding structure, the family, and you.

items.rule: NEVER deploy an AI tool school-wide without a data-handling contract appropriate to student data processing.
items.why: UU PDP compliance burden rests on the school. Deploying a consumer-tier tool at scale creates a documented compliance gap.
items.safeAlternative: School-tier products with explicit no-training-on-school-data terms. Document the contract in your approved-tools register.

items.rule: NEVER permit AI to grade summative work or contribute to reported grades.
items.why: Cambridge coursework integrity + Eduversal academic integrity policy + AI is not reliable at evaluating understanding.
items.safeAlternative: AI drafts feedback templates that teachers adapt. Moderation remains human.

items.rule: NEVER submit an inflated institutional self-assessment to the Academic Directorate.
items.why: Cross-school comparison data exposes inflation immediately. The cost is reputational and undermines your school's case for resources where you genuinely need support.
items.safeAlternative: Honest baseline + realistic two-domain progression target. Schools at Level 2 honestly are better positioned than schools claiming Level 4 indefensibly.

items.rule: NEVER use AI to draft or grade staff appraisal content (KPI scoring, Appraisal v2.1 F-item ratings, principal evaluation).
items.why: Staff evaluation is the highest-stakes human judgement in school leadership. AI assistance contaminates the process and may breach data-handling norms for staff records.
items.safeAlternative: AI may help structure your own preparation notes. Final ratings and written feedback are human, in your voice.

items.rule: NEVER write school AI policy that contradicts or weakens Cambridge International's coursework AI guidance.
items.why: Coursework integrity is adjudicated by Cambridge. Your school's authority on this is to be MORE conservative than Cambridge, never less.
items.safeAlternative: If a Cambridge guidance is unclear, escalate to your Cambridge Coordinator, then to Cambridge International directly.

shouldOurSchoolAdoptThisTool.summary: Five-question check before adopting any AI tool school-wide
questions: Does the vendor offer a school-tier contract that excludes our data from model training? (If no, stop here.)
Where does the tool process data? Inside Indonesia, or cross-border with documented adequacy safeguards? (If neither, stop or escalate.)
Does the tool meet a current school priority, or is it a solution looking for a problem? (If the latter, defer.)
Do we have the technical support capacity to deploy and maintain it? (If no, smaller pilot first.)
Is the cost justified by expected impact, or are we paying for a logo? (Be honest.)

staffMemberMisusedAi.summary: What to do when a staff member breaches AI policy
branches: First incident, low harm (e.g. used personal ChatGPT to draft a lesson plan with no student data): coaching conversation, document.
First incident, moderate harm (e.g. entered student names into a public AI tool): formal warning, immediate retraining, document.
Repeated incidents OR high harm (e.g. AI-generated summative grading, deliberate misrepresentation of AI use): follow normal performance management process per school HR policy.
In all cases: refer to Eduversal AI Competency Framework v1.0 and your school's specific policy as the cited authority, not your own judgement.

studentAcademicIntegrityIncident.summary: When a student submits suspected AI-generated work
branches: For non-Cambridge work: follow your school's academic integrity policy. Don't accuse on detector-tool output alone.
For Cambridge coursework: defer to Cambridge International's process via the Exams Officers Guide Phase 3. Do not improvise.
In all cases: have a conversation with the student first. False positives are common, especially for EAL writers.

progressionPath._note: Leaders progress in two parallel tracks: as practitioners (your own AI use, see teacher-playbook.json) and as institutional leaders (advancing your school's maturity, this playbook).
year_1_realistic_target: School AI policy adopted and communicated.
Majority of teaching staff at Foundation level competency.
Two domains identified for advancement in Year 2.
year_2_realistic_target: Two targeted domains advanced one maturity level (evidenced, not claimed).
Majority of teaching staff progressing toward Practitioner level.
Student AI literacy curriculum implemented systematically.
Annual policy review completed with documented changes.
year_3_aspirational: School at Established (Level 3) on most domains, Advanced (Level 4) on the two targeted in Year 2.
Identified teacher leaders contributing to network professional development.
Evidence of AI integration impact on student outcomes (not just claimed impact).
progressionPath.selfAssessmentRef: Annual self-assessment (Phase 2c-2d teacher level + Phase 3a institutional level) writes to Firestore. Eduversal Academic Directorate appraises during network visits.

additionalResponsibilities: AI strategy alignment with foundation-level priorities and budget.
Liaison with Eduversal Academic Directorate on network-level AI direction.
Approval of major AI tool adoptions with significant budget implications.
Annual report to foundation board on AI integration progress and risks.
boundaries: You set strategic direction; the Principal owns daily execution.
You are not the school's AI integrity adjudicator — that's the academic structure.

additionalResponsibilities: Whole-school AI integration is a Principal accountability.
Communication of AI policy to staff, students, parents.
Final escalation point for AI policy disputes within school.
boundaries: Defer to Cambridge on coursework AI integrity.
Defer to Foundation Rep on foundation-level strategic decisions and major budget.
Defer to Eduversal Academic Directorate on network-level standards.

additionalResponsibilities: Operational ownership of teacher AI competency development.
Coordination of student AI literacy curriculum across departments.
Aggregation of teacher self-assessments for school-level reporting.
Identification of practitioner-level teacher leaders for mentor roles.
boundaries: You coordinate, you don't grade. Teacher AI competency level is the line manager's call (typically Subject Leader for departmental teachers).
Defer to Cambridge Coordinator on syllabus-specific AI integration questions.

additionalResponsibilities: Active monitoring of Cambridge International AI hub updates.
Termly briefing of department leaders on Cambridge AI guidance changes.
Coursework AI integrity case lead (in partnership with Exams Officer).
Liaison with Cambridge International on local AI-implementation questions.
boundaries: You interpret Cambridge guidance; you don't write parallel school policy that diverges from it.
You are not the school's general AI policy authority — that's the Principal + Academic Coordinator.

pairedDocuments.teacherPlaybook: teacher-playbook.json — read this to understand what your teaching staff are working with
pairedDocuments.specialistPlaybook: specialist-playbook.json — read this if you interact with HQ subject specialists on curriculum or assessment
pairedDocuments.promptLibrary: prompt-library.json — leadership-relevant prompts (policy drafting, parent comms, meeting summarisation)
pairedDocuments.decisionTrees: decision-trees.json — full flowcharts for the decisionAids summaries above

openQuestions._note: Questions this playbook intentionally does not answer.
items: Network-level approved-AI-tools list for 2026-2027 — to be confirmed by Eduversal Academic Directorate.
How institutional AI maturity ratings interact with network resource allocation — to be defined by Academic Directorate.
Whether Eduversal will mandate a single school-tier AI vendor across the network, or leave selection to schools — open.
Indonesian Permendikdasmen AI-USE regulation — none exists as of 2026-05-16. The framework v1.0 fills this gap for now; if a regulation is published, alignment work will be required.
